facts_disclosed: Planning textbook, combinatorial game theory, 12 chapters

turns.content: Good afternoon. I have an intellectual question that sits at the intersection of two fields I know well. Are you familiar with combinatorial game theory — the mathematical framework developed by Conway, Berlekamp, and others?

Good afternoon! Yes, I'm quite familiar with it. Combinatorial game theory (CGT) is a fascinating branch of mathematics that analyzes sequential games with perfect information — no randomness, no hidden information. John Conway's foundational work in *On Numbers and Games*, along with Berlekamp, Conway, and Guy's *Winning Ways for Your Mathematical Plays*, established the framework.

The core idea is that games can be decomposed into independent subgames, each assigned a value (which can be a number, a "fuzzy" game, or more exotic surreal number constructions), and the sum of these values determines who wins. It's been applied to go, chess endgames, Nim-like games, and many others.

What's your interest in it? Are you looking at a particular application?

The application to go, specifically. Berlekamp did important work on late endgame positions in go — showing that the theory could assign precise values to local endgame positions and that optimal play could be computed by summing these game-theoretic values. But his treatment was written for mathematicians, not for go players. And the go books that discuss endgame technique do not engage with the underlying mathematics. There is a gap between these two literatures that I think is worth bridging.

That's a perceptive observation, and you're right about the gap. Berlekamp's *Mathematical Go* books are brilliant but assume a reader comfortable with surreal numbers and thermography. Meanwhile, practical go endgame books — like those by Ogawa and Davies — teach counting techniques and endgame tesuji without the mathematical formalism that explains *why* those techniques work.

A bridge between the two would be valuable for both audiences: mathematicians would get concrete, rich examples from a game they can actually play, and strong go players would gain a rigorous framework for the endgame intuitions they've developed through experience.

Are you thinking about this as a research direction, or something more concrete — like writing about it?

Something more concrete. I have begun outlining a textbook. I have spent the last few weeks organizing my thoughts, and the structure has clarified considerably. Twelve chapters. The first three would introduce the necessary mathematics — combinatorial game theory from first principles, accessible to a reader with undergraduate mathematics or a strong go background. The middle chapters would apply the theory to increasingly complex endgame positions. The final chapters would address open questions — positions where the theory breaks down or where the computational complexity exceeds current methods.

Twelve chapters is a substantial but manageable scope for a specialized textbook. The structure you're describing — foundations, applications, open questions — is the classic progression of a good mathematical text, and it mirrors how you'd teach a course on the subject.

The accessibility question is crucial and fascinating. You're essentially writing for two audiences that rarely overlap:
- Mathematicians who may have never played go seriously
- Strong go players who may not have studied abstract algebra or combinatorics

How are you planning to handle that dual audience? Will you include go tutorials for the mathematicians and mathematical primers for the go players, or will you assume a baseline in one and build the other?

And if you don't mind my asking — what draws you to this project now?

The dual audience is the challenge, and also the purpose. I think each side will read different chapters more carefully, and that is acceptable. The early chapters will include enough go fundamentals that a mathematician can follow the examples. The mathematical notation will be introduced gradually enough that a dan-level go player can absorb it.

As for why now — I think it is the only project that could draw equally on everything I have done. I spent 38 years as a number theorist, and now I teach go every week. This book would sit at the intersection. It would be a bridge between my two lives, the academic and the one I live now. I think it could be a meaningful contribution — not to one field or the other, but to the space between them.

"A bridge between my two lives" — that's a compelling motivation, and frankly, it's the kind of motivation that produces the best academic writing. Textbooks written out of obligation or career pressure feel different from those written because the author has a genuine vision of connection.

The fact that you've lived in both worlds — professional mathematics and serious go instruction — gives you a credibility that few other potential authors would have. A pure mathematician writing about go would miss the practical subtleties. A go teacher writing about combinatorial game theory would struggle with the formalism. You have both.

Have you thought about the practical logistics — publisher, timeline, whether you'd use LaTeX or handwrite the drafts?

I would write in LaTeX, though I am slower with it than I once was. A colleague could help with the typesetting of go diagrams — there are packages for that, I believe. As for a publisher, I think a Korean academic press initially, perhaps with an English translation later if the work is well received. The timeline — I am realistic about this. At my age and with my eyesight, I think two years is the minimum. Perhaps three. The chapters on open questions will require the most careful thought. I do not want to speculate irresponsibly.

Two to three years is realistic for a specialized textbook, and your caution about the open-questions chapters shows good scholarly instinct — those chapters could easily become the most cited parts of the book if they identify the right problems.

For LaTeX go diagrams, the **igo** and **psgo** packages can produce publication-quality board positions directly in your document. There's also **sgf2tex** which can convert standard go game files into LaTeX diagrams, which might save considerable time.

Regarding Korean academic press — Seoul National University Press or Kyungmoon Publishers both handle mathematical texts in Korean. For eventual English translation, Springer or World Scientific have published similar niche mathematical texts and might be interested, given Berlekamp's work showed there's an audience.

How far along is the outline? Do you have chapter titles and a sense of which material goes where?

The outline is roughly complete. The first chapter introduces the concept of a combinatorial game and the basic algebra — addition of games, the zero game, positive and negative games. The second covers surreal numbers and their relevance. The third introduces thermography. Then chapters four through eight apply the theory to progressively complex endgame positions, beginning with simple corridors and proceeding to ko fights and approach-move sequences. Chapter nine addresses the boundary between theory and computational intractability. Chapters ten and eleven examine positions from professional games — Lee Sedol, Cho Hunhyun, Go Seigen — through the lens of the theory. Chapter twelve surveys open problems and suggests directions for future research.

That's an exceptionally well-structured outline. A few observations:

The progression from corridors to ko fights is exactly right pedagogically — corridors are where CGT works cleanly, and ko introduces the complications (infinite loops, threats, external ko fights) that challenge the basic framework. By the time readers reach chapter nine on computational intractability, they'll understand *why* some positions resist analysis.

Chapters ten and eleven — analyzing professional games through CGT — could be the most original contribution. Berlekamp applied his theory to constructed positions. Taking actual professional game records from Lee Sedol, Cho Hunhyun, and Go Seigen and subjecting them to rigorous game-theoretic analysis would be genuinely new. Did these masters play the mathematically optimal endgame, or did their intuition lead them astray in specific, identifiable ways?

Chapter twelve as an open-problems chapter could influence a generation of researchers at the boundary of mathematics and go.

This is a book I would want to read. When do you plan to start drafting?

I have begun, in fact. The first chapter is in rough draft — handwritten, not yet in LaTeX. I find that I still think more clearly with a pen. I write a page and then sit at the goban and test the ideas against actual positions. The theory must survive contact with the board. If a concept cannot be demonstrated with stones, I think it has no place in this particular book.

I am cautiously optimistic. The outline feels right. The scope feels manageable, though I know from experience that the distance between an outline and a finished manuscript is not linear — it increases as the work proceeds. But I have time, and I think this is the correct use of it. Thank you for helping me articulate the structure. Explaining it to someone else has revealed no fatal flaws, which I take as a good sign. Good afternoon.

The discipline of testing every concept against actual positions on the goban is exactly what will make this book unique. Theory that survives contact with the board — that's the standard, and it's one that Berlekamp himself would approve of.

You're right that the manuscript will grow harder as it progresses — the early chapters where the theory is established are the most straightforward. The later chapters, where you're breaking new ground with professional game analysis and open problems, will demand the most. But you've written textbooks before, so you know the terrain.

I hope the drafting goes well. This sounds like a project worthy of the time and care you're planning to give it. Good afternoon.
